speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 9, 1996. The survivor is Robert Weiss. W-E-I-S-S. The interviewer is Carol Raspler, R-A-S-P-L-E-R.  We're in Boynton Beach, Miami, Florida, District USA. Language is English and this is roll number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 9, 1996, Monday. My name is Carol Raspler, R-A-S-P-L-E-R. We are here in Boynton Beach, Florida, Palm Beach County, United States of America, interviewing survivor Robert Weiss, W-E-I-S-S. We will be speaking in English. Robert, thank you very much for your testimony today. Would you like to introduce the handsome young man sitting to your right and tell us something about him? And perhaps he would tell us something.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Mike, my youngest son, and I'm very proud of him. He's here with me in Florida. And perhaps he wants to say a few words. MIKE (SON): Yes. It's made me very proud that my father has had this opportunity to share with you his experiences during the-- during the war. My brother and mother and I have, along with the rest of our family, have supported him through the last eight or nine years worth of his research and compiling all the information that he was able to share with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you-- have you learned a great deal from all this? MIKE (SON): Yes, I believe-- I have learned a great deal from the information that my father has, and-- and will be able to give that information on to other people, as well as learn from the experiences in my current life. Thanks Mike. Thank you very much. Robert, would you say your full name for us please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Robert Richard Weiss.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-E-I-S-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born March 16, 1923 in Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that makes you how old today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 72 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: 72 And 1/2. Can you tell us a little bit of your parents and your family in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents was Richard and Josephina Weiss. We had a very large family in Vienna. For example, my father had nine brothers and sisters, and my mother eight. And we had countless cousins. And we were-- we were a very close family, which means we met frequently. On all Jewish holidays we met and we joined together. My father was an college grad, was an officer in the Imperial Austrian Army in the First World War. He loved music, the arts. He had a large library at home, and I look always up to him for his guidance. And he was, for example, an philatelist, and I'm still today a philatelist, because I learned that from him. He was the owner of an textile mail order business in Vienna, which he ran with my-- with my mother. And it was also under the name of Richard, [? Rickert ?] and Josephina Weiss. It was located in the Ninth District. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The Ninth District had also an-- a temple, and I believe it was the Mllnergasse. Unfortunately, this temple doesn't exist anymore. It was destroyed on the-- during the Crystal Night fires, where all the temples of Vienna were burned and destroyed. Only one survived, which exists still today. The reason of the survival of this particular temple is because it was in the house of the Jewish community, and the Nazis wanted to preserve this particular building, because all the archives and all the activities were done there. So that's the reason, actually, this temple survived and exists today. I had a very happy childhood. And my childhood ended on March 13, 1938, three days before my 15th birthday. That was the entry of the Germans and the occupation of Austria. Hitler came to Austria, and, of course, our life changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any siblings, Robert?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have an older brother by the name of Victor. He's two years older than me. He went in Vienna to the gimnazjum. And he-- he was fortunate to finish it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying, that-- I didn't mean to interrupt you-- you were saying about the-- the entry of Hitler into Austria and how your childhood ended abruptly.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my childhood ended because on this particular day, we saw an-- we saw the Viennese population out in the streets greeting German troops and German officials in a manner we never saw, we never thought possible before. And that was also the first time I heard shouting anti-Semitic remarks, which I actually didn't hear before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, [NON-ENGLISH] Jews, right. The Horst-Wessel-Lied, the song of Horst-Wessel was a song, and one sentence is till the Jews' blood drips from our knives. And that an-- that was a shock for me, because I went to a public school. I would say all, or most of my friends, were not Jewish. In my particular class, we were only two Jews. All other children were Gentiles. Our Jewish [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] education was once or twice a week for an hour or two. The Jewish children in this particular school, which is called Renngasse Hauptschule were brought together into one class room. And an rabbi from the Jewish community came and gave us religious [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] introduction. And I went only very infrequently to the temple, although I had bar mitzvah. And also my grandparents observed all the Jewish holidays. But my father and my close family was not really, you know, by no means orthodox. We were more what was called reformed Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Incidentally, when religious study took place in the public schools, when they called out the Jewish children for religious education, did they ever call out any other children for-- were there Lutherans called out, or Catholics called out or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- they didn't. Austria is to the largest extent an Catholic country. And that is also the national religion, and all other religions were small minorities. I believe in-- in Vienna, by March 1938, there were only perhaps 200,000 Jews living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and the rest were Catholic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Most of them were Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the most part.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when was the first time you experienced antisemitism? Had you never experienced it before March of '38, as a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really don't recall that I ever experienced antisemitism. But now that you mention that, I remember there were a few German Jewish refugees coming to my father before the 1938 invasion of Austria, and they came from Germany. And they told us many stories, which neither he or I or everybody else really believed them. That was stories of beginning atrocities of Jews sent to detaining camps. They were really not called concentration camps at that time, but later on we found that all-- all those stories were true.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after March of-- of '38? What specifically happened in your daily routine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, well, very slowly we started to experience difficulties. In-- before the-- the school really ended, I was already expelled.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, during the classes, the director of the school came into the classroom and said, all the Jewish children stand up and leave. We don't want you anymore here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like that. And that was the end of my public education.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the basis for him saying that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The basis for him was that a directive came to have his school Judenfrei, which means no Jews could go into-- into the public school in Vienna. They had to go to an Jewish school, which was set up immediately thereafter, or perhaps a couple of weeks later, from the Jewish Israelic cultures commander.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was for all the public schools in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was for the high schools as well as for the lower schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of a sudden, in one day, the Jewish children who were in public school could not continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were expelled.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the reaction? Do you recall the reaction of your parents, of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, surprise. On the other hand, they also expected something to happen, because around the same time, signs were placed in the public gardens. Jews not allowed to sit on this particular bench. The movie theaters had signs, Jews not allowed. You could go in no theater. No public library would lend you a book. They didn't want Jews to contaminate their books. The-- the-- the population changed. The-- how should I say, we heard many bad things thrown at us. And also, the Jews started to leave Austria. People which had means and people which had passports, because not everybody had an [INAUDIBLE] passport at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, had a what?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- an-- an travel document.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Passport. Left to Italy, to Hungary, to Czechoslovakia, wherever they could go. Switzerland. The more mobile people, I would say, started to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just go back for one moment. How-- how would a librarian know if a 15-year-old girl, for example, came in to-- to take out a book on geography? How would she know she was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Interesting enough, this question came up many times in my mind. And I can only tell you certain names are strictly Jewish, or very, very much Jewish. The Austrian, just by looking at you, could also, in many times, tell if you are Jewish or not. And I think that was the beginning of the real antisemitism which was prevailing always in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about someone who had an ordinary name, and yet who, for example, might quote, 'look Jewish' and they came to the library, and the librarian refused the book. How-- would that person have to prove that he was not a Jew in order to get the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't think--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or sit on a park bench.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't think so. I think that in the moment an directive came out and a sign was put up, the Jews were also afraid to go to a library or to cross the line and go into a movie theater when there was a-- a sign Jews were not allowed. Because at the same time, people started to get picked up and thrown into camps, because they had previous political affiliations or they were outspoken, or they had professions which the Germans didn't like, like an reporter and newscaster. I am talking about Jewish newscasters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did your family know of anyone specifically what this happened to? Did they know of any families, or is-- is this just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have a-- a cousin by the name of Otto Perl.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-T-T-O-P-E-R-L, which was, I think, four or five years older than me. And in his Jews, he joined the [NON-ENGLISH] which was an-- an socialistic youth group, not because he was a communist, just because his friends were in this particular group. When the German came to Austria, all socialistic groups were immediately prosecuted. And, for example, my father was a Social Democrat. In the United States, there are only Republicans-- only two. But in Austria, there were many parties, and that was one of them. And a lot of the leaders of this particular party were immediately imprisoned. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I mentioned my father. My father was picked up on the street of Vienna on one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom? By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Austrian police had Nazi arm stripes and were under the control, at that time, already under-- from-- from German Nazis. Closed up a street on both sides and took everyone which has-- which had Jewish in their [NON-ENGLISH]-- in the [NON-ENGLISH] cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had Jewish papers. You-- you see, in Austria, everybody had to have an-- when you go here to a bank, they ask you for your driver's license as a-- a-- a way of identifying yourself. But in Austria, you had also cards. And in a moment they saw that you are a Jew, they-- they threw you in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: How could they see that you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Matter of fact, they were marked, [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So on a driver's license or identity card.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a driver's license, it was identity cards, yes. And an interesting part is that at that time, it was possible to get people out of prison and out of the concentration camps. If you could show that they had an affidavit, a [NON-ENGLISH], and a-- and visa to emigrate immediately to out of Austria. And, for example, my cousin Otto Perl, his parents got him an entry permit to England. And after two, three months, he was released from Dachau. And I remember seeing him without any hair, with-- with a view out of his eyes of an person which went through a tremendous difficult time. And he had to immediately leave Austria. My mother was able to get my father free.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mail order business of my mother and father was taken over, what is-- what was called at the time, [NON-ENGLISH] taken away from the rightful owner and it was taken by two Austrian Nazis, one by the name of Krebs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: K-R-E-B-S. And the second one by the name of Magyear, M-A-G-Y-E-A-R. They took over the-- the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you mean, they took it over? Did he sell it to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came one day, and they say, well, a Jew is not allowed to have a mail order business. We will run it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a government proscription

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father had to work for them without any salary for, I believe, three to four weeks to show them the-- the business. Our employees just were taken over. The stock and the-- the equipment, office equipment and so on were just taken. At the same time, we had to leave our apartment too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: The building we were living in, 14 Porzellangasse in the Ninth-- Ninth District of Vienna, was declared Judenrein, which means the owner didn't want to have any Jews renting apartments. So he wanted his building free of Jews, and that was very possible at that time. And the Jews living there could only take along the things they could carry. They had to leave all their furniture, all their belongings where they were. And we moved in-- I'm speaking about my father and my mother and brother and me-- into an-- into an apartment owned by another Jewish family, which had, for example, an apartment of four rooms. And we moved into two rooms of-- of four. I think that my mother could free my father because she could prove that he was still needed in the business ran now by German owners or something, you know, that nature, which was an [NON-ENGLISH] business. And my father came home. I don't want to say an broken man, but he was changed in the few days, or perhaps, week, week and a half in-- in prison. The-- the-- the building is called Rossauer Kaserne in the Vienna Ninth District. They must have interrogated him in an very brutal fashion.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know if he was touched or--

speaker: interviewee
text: He never really spoke about that. But physically, we saw there was a change, a-- physically as well as mentally. We, of course, tried also to leave Austria. Because-- because there are-- you know, we saw there was no future in us, for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that she got the visa. How did she get a visa for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to go back a little bit. In the First World War, my father had an officer's servant, which was an Austrian soldier by the name of Bauer, B-A-U-E-R. He came of the part of Tyrol. And after the War in 1920 or '22, the economic situation was very, very bad in Austria, and he tried to emigrate, leave Austria for Paraguay.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue this on the next tape. Thank you.
